speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five. Today is January 2, 1997. The survivor being interviewed is Janet Singer Applefield. The interviewer is Beth Cohen. The interview is being conducted in Plymouth, Massachusetts, United States of America. The language of the interview is English. Janet, can you describe who's in the picture, where, and when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that, I believe, was taken in the mid-'30s. On the left is my Uncle Alec. He's married to my Aunt Liba, next to him. And next to my Aunt Liba is my Grandmother Nabel and my grandfather, Jakob Nabel. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my parents' wedding day. They were married in 1934. On-- on the left, as we're looking at the picture, are my grandparents, Singers. And on the right of my father is my grandmother. And my grandfather is standing way in the back. And it's the rest of the family. Aunts, uncles and cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was-- they were married in-- I think they were married in Krakow, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is a picture of my mother, on the left, and her sister, Liba, on the right. They're standing in front of my grandfather's store. It says Singer-- E. Singer, I think. His name was Emmanuel. And that was taken, oh, in the mid-'30s, I think, in Nowy Targ, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] That's a photograph of my mother and me, when I was born in June 4, 1935, in Krakow, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] My parents. My father's holding me. We're standing on a balcony in Nowy Targ, Poland. And I must be just a few months old. I don't how old there. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a photograph of my mother and father holding my baby sister, Sarenka. It's the only picture I have of her. She was just an infant and this was taken in-- I think in 1940. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This photograph was taken in Nowy Targ in our apartment. My mother and father-- I'm sitting on my mother's lap. My Grandmother Singer's sitting next to my mother. And my aunt is on the right and my cousins are on the left. And that was taken-- I-- my guess is 1938 or '39, prior to the war. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is the cottage where I lived with my cousin, Lala, in Myslenice during the war. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is a picture of me, on the left, with two little friends when I lived on the farm. We're sitting on the stoop of the church, a wooden church. And that was-- my guess is 1943. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is a photo of me being held by Helena Golomp when I was living on the farm. And this is in the orchard, where there were fruit trees and gardens. And she's holding me. And once again, I think that was about 1943, '44. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This photo-- these two photos were taken during the war of my parents. You could see the tremendous change that has taken place in the way they look. I imagine this-- the year is 1942 or '43, something like that. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is a photo of Alicia and Ludwig Golomp. I think this was taken way pre-war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I really don't know the date but probably in the '20s. And-- and they-- Alicia is the woman who took in-- took me from the street and saved me. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is a picture of my confirmation. I'm the third one from the right in the top row. And this must have taken place in 1945 in Krakow, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself and my father taken in Paris, France, and in the winter of 1947, just prior to our trip to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] A picture of my-- me and my father taken on the boat that we came on March 15, 1947, the And we came-- we came to New York from-- from England. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] This is a photo of myself, on the left, and Janina, a Polish woman who helped me out during the war when my cousin used to, you know, abuse me and beat me up. Janina used to bandage me up and feed me and-- and took care of me. And this was-- well, this photo was taken [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about four years ago, 1993. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The picture on the-- on this-- on the left was taken in 1947, just prior to coming to the United States. I was 10 years old. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then the next photo is my-- where I-- after I came to this country, my eighth grade graduation. So I think I was 12 or 13 there. And the next one is my high school graduation in 1953. And the last photo is my engagement picture in 1954, taken 1954. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to introduce my husband, Jerry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my oldest son, David, in the red.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter, Debbie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: My youngest son, Jonathan. My son-in-law, Craig.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Milley. Julia, where are you? And Julia, my daughter-in-law. And Alexandre, my grandson. Anna-Amalia, my granddaughter. And the newest member of the family, Jakob Applefield-Milley. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyone care to start the dialogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why don't I? Well, it's been interesting. It's been an experience that one would have to say, of admiration. The attitude that Janet has maintained over the years and the achievement that she has attained have been incredible. We don't always agree on-- in terms of the Holocaust. Her position is one of much more acceptance of the German nation and looking for the good. And for that I admire her. I don't always agree with her. But I think her achievement over the years has-- has been most admirable. Somebody else? David you're next in the lineage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing I would like to add is that I feel that the story of how my mother and her parents lived and came through, has been in a very indelible way passed on because it's part of our cultural tradition and part of our direct history. And I think the fact that Julie and I have named our children after our-- well, Anna-Amalia is-- Amalia is my mother's mom that we never knew. And Alexandre, comes from Alojzy, which is Alexander Singer, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] father of-- of my mother who had a strong influence on-- on us as we grew up. And I think that's a lot of the experience of being children of a survivor of the Holocaust has influenced the way we see the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: And your book.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I wrote a novel called which is coming out very soon. And it's pretty much based on the history of my mother and her father but brought forward a generation. I had-- had it set in contemporary America. And I think the fact that I was compelled to write a-- a book based on a story indicates the-- the influence and the importance and certainly the presence in today. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My experience in regard to growing up knowing what my mother had gone through and all that she has become-- I feel very, very lucky that my mother has, for the past 36 years, nurtured an incredible mother-daughter relationship with me. And I certainly aspire to be the kind of mother to my son that my mother has been to me, especially in light of the adversity and-- and the horrific experiences that she went through as a child. And I think the biggest thing for me is that I feel under no circumstances does my mother ever deserve to be in pain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just knowing what-- and seeing, you know, the films that we watch, and growing up knowing the stories. And just the whole story of her family. It sort of puts everything into perspective, of the-- what's important. And I feel that as a family we're really very honored and blessed to have her with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I love my mother very much, and I'm happy she was saved and that she has maintained a very generous and compassionate view towards [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people in general. She's very gracious and helpful. You know, she's very into helping others, and I think that's because of her own experiences of being [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hidden and then cared for and-- and protected. And-- and I love her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- when I first met Deb and she told me of the story of her mother and her flight to freedom, I was truly amazed. And being a non-Jew, it has really heightened my sensitivity towards prejudice and racism around the world. And you can be assured that Jakob will know the story of his grandmother for years to come. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING FRENCH] We lived in France so we speak French and English as well. And not sure if Alexandre or Julia--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't have to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think you all said everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody said everything!
